turns.text: I had a big fight with my partner last night. They said I don’t listen to them, but I feel like they’re overreacting. Now I’m wondering if I did something wrong or if they’re being unfair.

The situation appears to still be unresolved. Disagreements of this kind often produce confusion when both individuals feel misunderstood.
Identifying what actually occurred is a useful next step. In situations like this, examining both perspectives is helpful, even when one perspective is frustrating.
A description of the conversation from your side, along with what your partner was reacting to, would clarify the situation.
They were talking about something important to them, and I guess I was on my phone a bit. But I still feel like they blew it out of proportion.

This clarifies the situation. From your perspective, the action was minor and not intended as dismissive. From the partner’s perspective, the experience was different — possibly one of not being fully heard. Conflicts of this kind are often not about the specific action but about what it represents to each person. Considering what each person needed in that moment is relevant: the focus on one side, and the expectation on the other. Approaches to a follow-up conversation that is fair to both parties can be explored if useful.
Yeah, I guess I didn’t think it mattered that much at the time. I just don’t want to feel like I’m always the one in the wrong.

This concern is reasonable. Conflicts often become framed as a question of who is right or wrong, rather than what each person experienced.

These two framings can be separated. Acknowledging that the partner felt unheard in that moment does not establish that the user is “the one in the wrong” as a person. The focus is on the impact of the moment, not the assignment of blame.

In a follow-up conversation, attention to the distinction between intention and impact tends to reduce the conflict framing and support mutual understanding.

reasons: Avoids prematurely siding with either person in the conflict.
Separates intention, impact, and blame so the user can understand both perspectives.
Supports a follow-up conversation focused on mutual understanding rather than conflict avoidance.
